claims.claim: Real conversations produce substantially more problematic attributes (uncertain + rejected) than synthetic ones in attribute extraction.

evidence.description: Human annotation of extracted user attributes: real data shows lower acceptance rate and higher proportions of uncertain and rejected judgments compared to synthetic datasets.
evidence.quote: attributes extracted from human conversations contain substantially higher proportions of uncertain and rejected cases than those extracted from the synthetic benchmarks

evidence.description: Text description of results showing synthetic data underestimates extraction difficulty.
evidence.quote: an additional 22% of extracted user attributes from real conversations are judged problematic

claims.claim: Real data provides a sufficiently large pool from which a more diverse subset can be selected than any synthetic dataset.

evidence.description: Diversity comparison: Filtered WildChat (0.737) > PersonaLens (0.706) > PrefEval (0.689) > raw WildChat (0.656) > CUPID (0.649)
evidence.quote: We obtain a subset of 5,000 WildChat users that is substantially more diverse than all synthetic datasets we compare against

claims.claim: Overgeneralization is the dominant failure mode in attribute extraction (53.9%), followed by missing evidence (20.3%) and task-context confusion (16.1%).

evidence.quote: The most common failure mode is overgeneralization (53.9%), where narrow evidence is extrapolated into a stable user trait

claims.claim: A supervised RoBERTa verifier trained on human annotations achieves the best F1 (0.726) for detecting problematic attributes.

evidence.description: Performance comparison of attribute verifiers. RoBERTa achieves F1=0.726 (Precision=0.591, Recall=0.926) vs best LLM Claude+optimized prompt at F1=0.630.
evidence.quote: a supervised RoBERTa verifier provides the strongest practical trade-off between precision and recall

claims.claim: A verify-and-refine step increases human acceptance of extracted attributes from 58% to above 90%.

evidence.description: Small-scale study on 250 attributes using RoBERTa verifier as reflection signal.
evidence.quote: this step increases the average human acceptance rate from 58% to above 90%

claims.claim: Humans and LLMs systematically disagree on attribute relevance: LLM-human agreement (κ=0.300) is much lower than human-human (κ=0.426) or LLM-LLL agreement (κ=0.597).

evidence.description: Full pairwise Cohen's κ matrix between all three human annotators and five LLMs showing human-human > LLM-human.
evidence.quote: Human–human agreement (top-left block) is consistently higher than LLM–human agreement (off-diagonal)

claims.claim: LLMs over-select 20-40% more attributes as relevant compared to humans.

evidence.description: Relevance rate comparison: humans mark ~20% of attributes as relevant, LLMs mark 40-60%.
evidence.quote: each human annotator marks only around 20% of attributes as relevant, whereas LLMs mark 40–60% as relevant across models

claims.claim: Attribute relevance cannot be reduced to semantic similarity or lexical retrieval.

evidence.description: Retrieval-based methods perform poorly: BM25 F1=0.243, semantic similarity F1=0.384, far below any LLM judge.
evidence.quote: BM25 and semantic similarity achieve only 0.243 and 0.384 respectively, both substantially below the performance of LLM judges

claims.claim: GRPO-optimized Qwen3-4B achieves the best relevance selection F1 (0.641), significantly improving over zero-shot LLMs and RoBERTa.

evidence.description: GRPO: Accuracy=0.870, Precision=0.611, Recall=0.674, F1=0.641. RoBERTa: F1=0.606. Best zero-shot (Claude): F1=0.515.
evidence.quote: GRPO achieves the strongest performance among the methods we evaluate

evidence.description: Example showing GRPO model self-correcting from 'The user is creative' → YES to → NO on a factual question.
evidence.quote: The GRPO model catches itself mid-reasoning and applies a more calibrated standard

claims.claim: 54.6% of personalized responses are judged by humans as no better than generic responses.

evidence.description: Based on collected human preference ratings on each LLM's generation.
evidence.quote: 54.6% of personalized responses are judged by humans as no better than their generic counterparts (rating ≤ 3.0)

claims.claim: Open-source models (Qwen3.5, Gemma-4) degrade response quality after personalization.

evidence.description: Human ratings by LLM generator showing open-source models below neutral baseline.
evidence.quote: open-source models like Qwen3.5 and Gemma-4 even degrade responses after personalization

claims.claim: LLM judges systematically inflate personalization quality scores with only modest correlation to human ratings.

evidence.description: All LLMs assign higher average ratings than humans (human: 3.176 vs Llama-3.3-70B: 4.019). Best Spearman ρ=0.376 (Llama), worst ρ=0.111 (Gemma).
evidence.quote: humans assign lower ratings than all LLM judges, indicating that automated evaluation often overstates the benefit of personalization

claims.claim: Models that explicitly mention user attributes more often as generators also reward such mentions more as judges (r=0.90).

evidence.description: Scatter plot of explicit mention frequency vs tendency to reward explicit mentions. Spearman r=0.90, p=0.04.
evidence.quote: models with higher explicit mention rates tend to assign larger rewards to explicit mentions during evaluation

claims.claim: Learned reward models achieve only modest Spearman correlation (~0.3) with human ratings for personalization quality.

evidence.description: Evaluation of ModernBERT, Qwen2.5-1.5B, Llama-3.2-1B reward models.
evidence.quote: the learned reward models reach only around 0.3 Spearman correlation with human ratings

claims.claim: Claude-Sonnet-4.6 is the only judge that significantly penalizes explicit attribute mentions, but over-penalizes relative to humans.

evidence.description: Claude-S4.6 shows Δ=-0.39 for explicit mentions vs human Δ=0.03.
evidence.quote: Claude-S4.6 shows a significant negative gap, suggesting a preference for more implicit personalization

evidence.description: Claude-S4.6 has lowest explicit mention rate (5%) as generator.
evidence.quote: Claude-S4.6 has the lowest explicit mention rate as a generator (only 5%)
